title: Using a spin-triplet encoding to enhance shuttling fidelities in Si/SiGe quantum wells
authors: Merritt P. R. Losert, S. N. Coppersmith, Mark Friesen
abstract: Spatial variations of the valley splitting in a quantum well present a key challenge for conveyor-mode shuttling of electron spins in Si/SiGe, giving rise to Landau-Zener-like excitations that cause leakage outside the qubit subspace. Here, we propose an unconventional two-electron qubit encoding, based on valley-singlet states, that is largely immune to Landau-Zener leakage processes. In contrast to single-electron spins, the shuttling fidelity actually improves for small valley splittings, in this case. We show that high fidelities can be achieved without applying any special procedures, such as fine-tuning of the shuttling path.
summary: The paper proposes an unconventional two-electron spin-triplet encoding for Si/SiGe quantum dots to mitigate errors caused by valley-splitting variations during spin shuttling. This scheme significantly outperforms conventional single-electron shuttling by suppressing Landau-Zener leakage. The study demonstrates that high fidelities are achievable even in the presence of significant alloy disorder.
main_problem: Spatial variations in valley splitting in Si/SiGe quantum wells cause Landau-Zener-like excitations during spin shuttling, leading to leakage from the qubit subspace.
main_result: A two-electron spin-triplet encoding is proposed that is largely immune to valley-induced leakage, achieving fidelities 2-3 orders of magnitude higher than single-electron schemes.
method: Numerical simulations using Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) with effective-mass and tight-binding models, combined with Schrieffer-Wolff transformations and time-evolution propagators.
model_or_system: Two-electron spin-triplet qubits in Si/SiGe heterostructure quantum wells using a conveyor-mode shuttling architecture with oscillating sinusoidal potentials.
key_observables: Trace infidelity, leakage error rates, valley splitting, and orbital excitation energies.
important_parameters: Shuttling velocity (1-100 m/s), valley splitting (1.5-300 ueV), orbital confinement (2-6 meV), and disorder strength (10-50 ueV).
main_assumptions: Assumes orbital splitting is much larger than valley splitting; excited orbital states are treated perturbatively; interface steps are treated as a weak perturbation.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate two-electron orbital energies, trace infidelities across different disorder and confinement levels, and the scaling of errors with shuttling velocity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of valley-induced leakage, proposes a two-electron encoding, details the Hamiltonian and leakage mechanisms (SOC and alloy disorder), presents numerical results comparing single vs. two-electron fidelity, and discusses error sources like charge noise and phonon relaxation.
why_interesting: It presents a novel encoding strategy to protect quantum information against environmental-induced leakage, which is a fundamental problem in open quantum systems and hardware-efficient quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Encoding schemes for high-fidelity conveyor-mode shuttling in silicon. (a) Cross-sectional schematic of the shut- tling device, including clavier gates (C) fabricated above a Si/SiGe quantum well. Time-varying electrostatic potentials create pockets that can carry electrons along the shuttling channel. (b) Realistic variations of the valley energy splitting Ev across a 50 × 50 nm2 region of heterostructure, gener- ated as in [61, 62]. (c) Energy levels versus position x of a single-electron dot. When the dot travels through a region with small valley splitting Ev, Landau-Zener (LZ) processes can excite the electron into an excited valley state. (d) En- ergies versus position x of...

Figure 2. Infidelities obtained when shuttling spin triplets (Tr. Inf.) for different disorder and confinement strengths. (a) Two-electron orbital energies as a function of the confinement strength Eorb,1e, computed using FCI methods. Here, we assume vertical fields of Fz = 1 and 10 mV/nm and do not include alloy disorder. (b) Ground-state and (c) first-excited-state orbital densities, for Eorb,1e = 2 meV and Fz = 1 mV/nm, again computed with no disorder. (d) Trace infidelities computed at a shuttling distance of 2 µm, for a variety of confinement and disorder strengths (Eorb,1e and σ∆0, respectively), assuming a shuttling velocity of v = 10 m/s.
